description: This figure displays the binary Fe-Al equilibrium phase diagram, with aluminum content plotted on both the top x-axis (weight percent, 0 to 100 wt%) and bottom x-axis (atomic percent, 0 to 100 at%), and temperature on the y-axis ranging from 200 °C to 1600 °C. Key phases identified include liquid phase (L), α-Fe (bcc Fe), γ-Fe (fcc Fe), intermetallic phases Fe₃Al (D0₃ structure), FeAl (B2 structure), FeAl₂, Fe₂Al₅, FeAl₃, and pure Al. Marked critical transition and invariant reaction temperatures include 1530 °C (pure Fe melting point), 1394 °C (α-Fe to γ-Fe transition), 912 °C (γ-Fe to α-Fe transition), 770 °C (magnetic transition for α-Fe), 660 °C (pure Al melting point), alongside other invariant temperatures at 1310, 1215, 1164, 1171, 1157, 1092, and 652 °C. The diagram demonstrates that Al forms a disordered solid solution in Fe up to ~11 wt% Al, with brittle intermetallic phases forming at higher Al concentrations, justifying Al content restrictions in low-density ferrous alloys to avoid embrittlement.

This chart plots the density (unit: g·cm⁻³) of two lightweight ferrous alloy systems as a function of Al content (unit: wt.%). The blue circular data points and fitted line correspond to the binary Fe-Al alloy system, while the red triangular data points and fitted line correspond to the Fe-Mn-Al-C system with 14 wt.% Mn. Density of both systems decreases nearly linearly with increasing Al content, showing that Al is highly effective in reducing the density of ferrous alloys: addition of 8 wt.% Al leads to ~10% density reduction. At the same Al content, Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys show slightly lower density than Fe-Al alloys, confirming the mild density-reducing effect of Mn. This figure supports the core strategy of Al and Mn alloying for developing low-density lightweight steels for automotive applications.

This horizontal bar chart compares the mean grain size (unit: μm) of several Fe-Al ferritic low-density steels against conventional Al-free interstitial-free (IF) steel. Four tested material systems are included: 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti steel with mean grain size ~18 μm, 8.1Al-0.0015C-0.14Ti steel with mean grain size ~92 μm, 6.8Al-0.0035C-0.10Ti steel with mean grain size ~70 μm, and reference IF steel with mean grain size ~18 μm. The comparison shows that low-density steels with ultralow carbon content and sufficient Ti stabilizer (6.8 wt.% Al and 8.1 wt.% Al steels) have much coarser grains than traditional IF steel, while the steel with higher C content and insufficient Ti stabilizer (9.7 wt.% Al steel) has similar fine grain size to IF steel. This figure supports the conclusion that free C in the matrix refines grains via the solute drag effect, while sufficient stabilizer addition for interstitial elements leads to grain coarsening in high Al ferritic low-density steels.

This bar chart demonstrates the bake hardening (BH) response of 5 Fe-Al ferritic steels after standard paint baking treatment (2% pre-deformation + 170 °C holding for 20 min). The nominal compositions (wt.%) of the 5 alloys are: 1 = 7Al-0.002C-0.01Ti, 2 = 7Al-0.004C-0.02Nb, 3 = 8Al-0.003C-0.01Ti-0.01Nb, 4 = 7.5Al-0.004C-0.02Nb, 5 = 6.5Al-0.005C-0.01Ti-0.01Nb. White bars represent pre-BH yield strength (YS, unit MPa), pink bars represent post-BH YS, and blue dots represent the YS increment (ΔYS) after BH treatment. ΔYS stays around 80 MPa for all 5 alloys, showing consistent bake hardening effect. Alloy 3 exhibits the highest pre-BH YS (~420 MPa) and post-BH YS (~500 MPa) among all samples. This figure confirms that properly designed high-Al low-carbon ferritic lightweight steels can achieve stable ~80 MPa yield strength improvement via BH treatment, originating from interstitial C atoms locking dislocations in the ferritic matrix, which satisfies the mechanical requirement for automotive lightweight applications.

This chart displays JMatPro 6.1-calculated equilibrium phase fractions (in weight percent) as a function of temperature from 0 °C to 1500 °C for an Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C (wt.%) lean duplex lightweight steel. Four phases are represented by distinct curves: ferrite (red), austenite (blue), carbides (purple), and liquid (black). Carbides are only stable below ~750 °C at very low fractions (<2 wt.%). Austenite forms at ~750 °C, reaches a peak fraction of ~10 wt.% near 900 °C, then gradually disappears above ~1300 °C. Ferrite is the dominant phase across most of the measured temperature range, dropping slightly to ~90 wt.% in the intercritical austenite stability region before recovering to nearly 100 wt.% until melting initiates at ~1450 °C. A key feature contrasting with conventional low-carbon steels is that austenite content decreases with increasing temperature in the intercritical region, validating the unique phase transformation behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex low-density steels.

This schematic illustrates the phase transformation sequence for κ-carbide formation from ferrite (α) in lean Mn-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels. The process follows sequential steps: first, austenite (γ) nucleates and grows from the ferrite matrix; second, a continuous ring of κ-carbide precipitates at the α/γ interphase boundary; third, the κ-carbide ring fragments and globulizes. After fragmentation, one or both of two transformation pathways occur: 1) total or partial eutectoid transformation of the remaining austenite to ferrite and κ-carbide, 2) formation of a modulated structure via spinodal decomposition of austenite into γ + κ-carbide. This figure demonstrates the multiple microstructure evolution routes of κ-carbide precipitation in low-Mn Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys during continuous cooling, supporting the phase evolution mechanism for triplex lightweight steel microstructure design and processing optimization.

This figure consists of two subplots illustrating the effect of alloying elements on the κ-phase solvus in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight ferrous alloys. Subplot (a) presents phase stability as a function of Al content (5 to 11 wt.%) in Fe-30Mn-xAl-1C alloys, with temperature (500 to 900 °C) as the y-axis. Three phase regions are marked: single γ (austenite) at high temperatures, γ + κ* (intergranular κ-carbide) at intermediate temperatures, and γ + κ + κ* (both intragranular and intergranular κ-carbide) at low temperatures. Solvus temperatures for both κ phases increase with rising Al content. Subplot (b) shows phase stability as a function of Mn content (23 to 30 wt.%) in Fe-xMn-7Al-1C alloys, with the same three phase regions; here, solvus temperatures decrease with rising Mn content. The figure confirms that Al elevates κ-carbide solvus while Mn reduces it, supporting optimized aging process design to tailor beneficial intragranular κ-carbide precipitation for improved mechanical performance of these lightweight steels.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterizations of microstructure evolution in Fe-(26-28)Mn-(11-12)Al-(0.9-1.15)C (wt.%) triplex lightweight steels after room temperature tensile deformation, to illustrate the shear band induced plasticity (SIP) effect. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field TEM image with a 2 μm scale bar, exhibiting shear bands formed on {111} planes of the austenitic matrix. Subfigure (b) is a dark-field TEM image collected using the (210) superlattice reflection of <001> fcc zone axis, with a 500 nm scale bar, showing the regular distribution of 20–30 nm sized κ-carbides that are coherent with the austenitic matrix. The observations confirm that uniformly dispersed coherent nano-sized κ-carbides promote homogeneous shear band formation in austenite during deformation, which is the core mechanism of the SIP effect that delivers excellent strength-ductility balance for these automotive lightweight steels.

This plot illustrates the relationship between ultimate tensile strength (x-axis, unit: MPa, range 0-1200) and total elongation (y-axis, unit: %, range 0-100) for multiple low-density lightweight ferrous alloy systems, with corresponding density reduction levels noted: Fe-Al ferritic steels achieve 7.5-12.7% density reduction, Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy series achieve 10-20% density reduction, and Fe-Al intermetallics achieve >25% density reduction. Five material performance regions are distinguished: red region for Fe-Al ferritic steels (UTS 500-650 MPa, elongation 15-28%), blue regions for four Fe-Mn-Al-C steel types: duplex steels (α+γ, UTS 650-800 MPa, elongation 35-48%), precipitation hardening (PH) steels (α+κ, UTS 780-920 MPa, elongation 8-20%), triplex steels (γ+α+κ, UTS 800-1100 MPa, elongation 42-75%), plus purple region for Fe-Al intermetallics (UTS 950-1050 MPa, elongation 0-10%). The figure demonstrates that austenite-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex and triplex steels exhibit superior strength-ductility balance and higher density reduction than ferritic steels, supporting their promising application potential for automotive lightweighting.

This figure presents engineering stress (MPa) versus engineering strain (%) tensile curves for four ferrous alloy systems, including three Al-containing low-density ferritic steels and a conventional interstitial-free (IF) steel as reference. The four curves correspond to: 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti (purple, ultimate tensile strength ~620 MPa, fracture engineering strain ~14%), 8.1Al-0.0015C-0.14Ti (blue, ultimate tensile strength ~520 MPa, fracture strain ~17%), 6.8Al-0.0035C-0.10Ti (red, ultimate tensile strength ~470 MPa, fracture strain ~32%), and IF steel (black, ultimate tensile strength ~300 MPa, fracture strain ~47%). The 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti alloy with higher free carbon content in its matrix exhibits distinct discontinuous yielding, while the other three alloys with lower or stabilized interstitial carbon show continuous yielding behavior. This figure supports the conclusion that free C in ferritic low-density steel matrix causes discontinuous yielding, which may lead to undesired surface stretcher marks during sheet forming, so interstitial C should be stabilized via alloying for these material systems.

This figure presents tensile mechanical test results for Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C MBIP austenitic low-density steel. It includes two curves plotted against true strain on the x-axis, with the y-axis representing strain hardening rate or true stress in MPa. The red true stress-true strain curve increases continuously with true strain to a peak of ~1600 MPa at a true strain of ~0.65, then drops after necking occurs. The blue strain hardening rate curve starts at a very high value (~2450 MPa) at small strain, first decreases to ~1550 MPa, then rises to a peak of ~1900 MPa at a true strain of ~0.45, and finally drops rapidly when true strain exceeds ~0.6. This figure confirms that austenite-containing low-density steels have an exceptionally high strain hardening rate, which is the core contributor to their excellent strength-ductility balance, distinguishing them from ferritic steels for automotive lightweighting applications.

This is a line plot illustrating the effect of Al addition (x-axis: Al content in wt.%, range 0 to 10) on Young's modulus (y-axis, unit GPa, range 140 to 220) of low-density ferrous alloys for automotive lightweighting applications. Two independent experimental datasets are presented: blue circular points with trendline from Rana et al. [6, 20], and red triangular points with trendline from Frommeyer et al. [18]. Both datasets exhibit a consistent monotonic decreasing trend of Young's modulus with increasing Al content: Rana et al.'s data shows Young's modulus reduces from ~205 GPa at 0 wt.% Al to ~153 GPa at 10 wt.% Al, while Frommeyer et al.'s data for 6 wt.% to ~8.5 wt.% Al shows modulus drops from ~190 GPa to ~167 GPa, matching the former trend. This figure verifies the negative side effect of Al alloying in low-density steels: while Al addition lowers steel density to enable vehicle weight reduction and improved fuel efficiency, it reduces Young's modulus which directly governs material stiffness, posing a challenge for application of these alloys in stiffness-critical automotive components.

This flow chart presents the typical laboratory-scale processing steps for producing Fe-Al-(C) and Fe-Al-Mn-C ferritic and multiphase lightweight sheet steels. The sequential steps include: ingot casting, reheating at 1250 °C, rough rolling, second reheating at 1250 °C, hot rolling with final thickness of 3.5 mm and finishing rolling temperature of 900 °C, run-out table cooling, coiling, pickling with HCl at 85 °C, cold rolling or warm rolling at 250 °C to a final thickness of 1 mm, continuous annealing at 850-900 °C, and aging at 400-700 °C. This route is compatible with existing industrial production lines for conventional automotive steels with no extra process steps required, and processing parameters can be adjusted based on alloy chemistry and desired microstructure. The figure proves the process feasibility of lightweight Fe-Al based steels with existing steel production infrastructure for potential industrial application.

This figure contains two subfigures showing finite element simulation results of shell thickness distributions for Volkswagen Golf V body-in-white (BIW) components under the constraint of constant overall torsional stiffness. Subfigure (a) presents the shell thickness of BIW parts made of conventional HSLA steels, with thickness ranging from 0.750 mm to 3.000 mm, color-coded by the provided legend. Subfigure (b) shows the shell thickness distribution when HSLA steels are replaced by a ferrite-based duplex low-density steel, with thickness ranging from 0.600 mm to 4.000 mm. Although the lower Young's modulus of the low-density steel leads to increased thickness of some components, the simulation predicts a 14.5% weight reduction of the whole BIW without compromising torsional stiffness. This figure supports the conclusion that low-density steels have significant lightweighting potential for automotive applications while maintaining required mechanical performance.
